User Stories from a Frontend Dev's Perspective

A follow-up after running through User Stories on a real project — the mindset really is opposite to traditional backend planning. Backend devs typically start with "what features and database columns do we need?" but User Stories start with "what does the user need?". This post is my notes from the first time I lived through that shift on a job-board example.

An interviewer asked me about User Stories and I realized I wasn't super clear on it — so I dug in and wrote a short note. A User Story captures a feature from the user's perspective ("As a [user], I want [something] so that [reason]") rather than from a database or implementation perspective. This post is the basics — the formula, a few real examples, and how it shifts how you think about building features.
